name: Pete Johnson
abstract: Kermit Holden "Pete" Johnson (March 25, 1904 – March 23, 1967) was an American boogie-woogie and jazz pianist. Tony Russell stated in his book The Blues – From Robert Johnson to Robert Cray that "Johnson shared with the other members of the 'Boogie Woogie Trio' the technical virtuosity and melodic fertility that can make this the most ex…

about_quotes.speaker: Samuel Lovett
about_quotes.evidence: I started playing with Buster Smith at a place called Lucille's Paradise and Baby Lovett, Herman Walder, Jack Johnson, a little place there and Pete Johnson... and I believe there were six members in that band

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: Dan Qualey... started a label called Solo Art which was the first to record Albert Ammons and Pete Johnson... He was the first – seemingly the first guy to take Nat under his wing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Thompson
about_quotes.evidence: Pete Johnson and Albert Ammons became very influential in my life, and they taught me the boogie-woogie style.

about_quotes.speaker: Wilbur Ware
about_quotes.evidence: And I don't know -- I hope I don't get in no trouble for saying it, but I met him, and we come back stage and the guys were saying just stand here, they were playing; they had just broadcasted -- they were broadcasting nightly -- and Sinatra was just talking to them; he said, "I'm Frank Sinatra" and
about_quotes.doc_title: 68088_Wilbur B. Ware, Sr oral history interview

about_quotes.speaker: Gene Ramey
about_quotes.evidence: Interviewer mentions Pete Johnson playing at the Lone Star; Interviewee confirms Pete Johnson was well known by then and discusses piano battles involving Everett Johnson and Pete Johnson, but does not describe a personal or professional relationship with Pete Johnson himself.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: The show had Hazel Scott, the Golden Gate Quartet, two sisters who danced East Indian dances named the Craft Sisters, Pete Johnson and Albert Ammons, the two boogie woogie pianists. With our trio of piano, bass and trumpet, we took care of the dancing.
